Over the past decade, Turkey has quietly become the world's busiest destination for hair restoration. The reasons are not mysterious: a dense concentration of specialised surgeons, a healthcare infrastructure built around international patients, and treatment packages that often cost a fraction of what the same procedure runs in Western Europe or North America. Add a city like Istanbul, where you can recover between two continents, with the Bosphorus on one side and centuries of history on the other, and it becomes clear why so many people turn a medical appointment into a genuine trip.But choosing a clinic from abroad is harder than choosing a hotel. You are trusting a team you have never met with a result you will carry for the rest of your life. This guide ranks ten of the most established hair transplant clinics in Istanbul and pairs the list with practical travel advice, so you can plan both the procedure and the days around it with confidence.The industry's premier medical R&D hub and pioneer of ultra-dense hair restoration.A multidisciplinary board of surgeons and biomedical researchers with over 15 years of combined clinical experience. Vera Clinic is not just a hair transplant centre; it is a recognised research and development institution that actively develops the industry's next-generation technologies. The team is behind innovations, including theCVD lab-grown diamond-tipped instrument for precision micro-incisions andfor maximum density. Named Best Hair Transplant Clinic in Turkey by the European Award in Medicine (2021) , and home to, which trains physicians and runs ongoing clinical research, the clinic gives patients access to patented, next-generation methods inside a five-star facility.Expert-led aesthetic restoration overseen by a Cleveland Clinic-trained plastic surgeon.Directed by Dr Ekrem Keskin, with extensive verified acclaim across major international medical review platforms.Capital Hair Center places a board-certified plastic surgeon at the helm of every procedure, bringing genuine precision to hairline design and facial harmony. Sapphire FUE and DHI are complemented by proprietary treatments like OzonFect and needle-free anaesthesia. A strong option for patients who want their transplant handled with the rigour of reconstructive surgery.A boutique clinic focused on personalised treatment and individual hairline design.Founded in 2016 by Şule Ölmez and supported by an experienced medical team.Unlike larger clinics that perform a high volume of procedures, Sule Hair Transplant emphasises a more personalised patient experience. The clinic offers Sapphire FUE and DHI techniques, with treatment plans tailored to each patient's goals and hair characteristics. Patients who value direct communication, detailed consultation, and a boutique environment often find the clinic's approach appealing. Its all-inclusive packages and structured aftercare programme also make the process straightforward for international visitors.A boutique approach pairing AI mapping technology with low daily patient volumes.A boutique team headed by Dr Merve Kaya.Este Favor focuses on lower daily intakes, so each case receives dedicated attention, pairing AI-assisted planning with the now-familiar combination of treatment plus travel coordination. It is a careful, detail-oriented option for medical tourists who want a smaller-scale experience.A long-standing, multidisciplinary institution with two decades of experience.Boasting a 4.8-star rating from thousands of verified reviews and global recognition in outlets.Aslı Tarcan Clinic draws on two decades of experience, covering Sapphire FUE, DHI, and Robotic DHI, with optional PRP and stem cell therapies. Multilingual staff, VIP transfers, and structured aftercare make it a solid choice for patients seeking a review-backed clinic with a long track record and all-inclusive setup.A straightforward, package-based experience for international medical tourists.An established Istanbul provider working in the international medical tourism space.Medart bundles the procedure with all the practical essentials, consultation, transfers, accommodation guidance, and aftercare that overseas patients look for. It's a strong pick for those comparing several mainstream Istanbul options and wanting a clear, predictable, all-in-one process.One of the largest hair transplant providers in Turkey, serving a high volume of international patients each year.Large multidisciplinary medical team and dedicated international patient services.Elithair has built its reputation around accessibility, structured treatment pathways, and experience managing patients from around the world. The clinic offers FUE-based hair restoration procedures supported by multilingual coordinators, comprehensive treatment packages, and established pre- and post-operative processes. With consultation locations in several countries and a large operational infrastructure, it is often considered by patients looking for a streamlined and highly organised treatment experience.Streamlined, end-to-end care built around the international patient.A coordinated team specialising in travel-and-treatment packages for overseas patients.AHD Clinic delivers a smooth, fully managed journey from initial consultation through aftercare. Its appeal lies in the seamless coordination that has become the Istanbul standard, ideal for patients who want a hassle-free, supported experience from start to finish.World-class Sapphire and DHI expertise on a high-volume scale.A high-volume international practice headed by Dr Levent Acar.Dr Acar is highly respected across Europe, and the clinic has built significant visibility in the international market. Custom-built for international patients, Cosmedica offers seamlessly structured all-inclusive packages using state-of-the-art techniques, making it a go-to for those comparing the larger, well-known practices.Full-service treatment paired with comprehensive logistical support.An Istanbul team focused primarily on serving overseas patients.Transmed combines the surgical procedure with airport transfers, accommodation guidance, and structured follow-up, simplifying the entire experience of travelling abroad for treatment. It suits patients who want both quality care and worry-free logistics in one package.Turkey's position as the world's leading hair transplant destination is not the result of marketing alone. It is built on decades of experience, high patient volumes, specialised medical teams, and continuous investment in hair restoration technologies.For international patients comparing options, the search for the best hair transplant clinic in Turkey often extends beyond price alone and includes factors such as surgeon involvement, patient support, and long-term results.As competition has increased, leading clinics have focused on improving patient outcomes, refining techniques, and delivering more natural-looking results. For patients willing to research their options carefully, Turkey continues to offer a combination of expertise, accessibility, and value that remains difficult to match elsewhere.
Article Summary: Turkey has quietly become the world's busiest destination for hair restoration. A dense concentration of specialised surgeons, a healthcare infrastructure built around international patients, and treatment packages that often cost a fraction of what the same procedure runs in Western Europe or North America. But choosing a clinic from abroad is harder than choosing a hotel.

Article Title: Sam Altman’s OpenAI launched Codex Micro, a limited-edition desktop keypad for AI agents
Article Text:
OpenAI Codex Micro : AI keypad for managing coding agents

OpenAI’s first hardware device could be a speaker

Sam Altman-led AI company OpenAI has launched Codex Micro, a limited-edition desktop keypad designed for developers who use its AI coding assistant, Codex. The device will offer users physical controls to monitor and manage multiple AI agents without switching between windows on a computer. Developed in partnership with hardware company Work Louder, the keypad features programmable keys, a rotary dial and a small joystick for quick AI actions.The device also includes a dedicated button for approving an AI agent's access to perform tasks, although some users may find that feature easier to activate accidentally.As stated above, Codex Micro is a compact desktop accessory that sits beside a user's keyboard and provides shortcuts for common Codex actions. The keypad includes backlit programmable keys, a push-to-talk button, a rotary dial to adjust AI reasoning settings and a joystick. It also displays the status of AI coding agents, allowing developers to track their progress at a glance.The device is aimed at Codex power users who rely on AI agents for software development rather than general ChatGPT conversations. The launch follows OpenAI's recent introduction of ChatGPT Work, which allows users to manage AI agents for tasks beyond coding without using the Codex app.Codex Micro is not the consumer AI hardware device OpenAI is widely expected to launch later this year. According to earlier reports, the company is developing a ChatGPT-powered smart home speaker that would compete with devices such as Amazon Alexa, Google Home and Apple HomePod. Earlier this week, Bloomberg reported that the speaker could be unveiled this year, with availability expected in 2027.
Article Summary: Codex Micro is a desktop keypad designed for developers who use OpenAI's AI coding assistant, Codex. The keypad features programmable keys, a rotary dial and a small joystick for quick AI actions. It also includes a dedicated button for approving an AI agent's access to perform tasks, although some users may find that feature easier to activate accidentally.

Article Title: Canva cofounder Cameron Adams on how to get employees to stop caring about AI: 'If you force a tool on them …
Article Text:
"If you force a tool on them, they're just going to do that very begrudgingly, and they're not going to enter into this very experimental mindset that we need them to,"

"So, we're not mandating Claude or ChatGPT or Gemini or whatever tool you want to use in your part of the company.

You can figure it out."

"The whole week, we said to people, 'Please don't do your normal work. We want you to think of the problems that you have, the tools that you've heard about, the opportunities that you've heard from colleagues in other industries, and we want you to try those out for the entire week,"

How AI adoption remains a challenge for companies



Canva cofounder Cameron Adams believes one of the fastest ways to cause employees to lose interest in AI is to force them to use a specific tool. In an interview with Rapid Response, Adams said Canva does not require employees to use a particular AI chatbot, such as Claude, ChatGPT, or Gemini. Instead, staff are free to choose the tools that best fit their work.Explaining why Canva avoids mandating a single AI platform, Adams said employees are more likely to experiment when they have the freedom to choose.he said.Adams added that employees are given dedicated AI budgets to experiment with different tools, build workflows and solve problems relevant to their roles. He said,According to Adams, Canva also sets aside time for employees to focus entirely on AI exploration. During the company's AI Discovery Week, staff were encouraged to pause their regular work to test AI tools and identify new use cases.he noted.Adams' comments come as many companies continue looking for ways to increase AI adoption among employees while managing costs.Some companies have introduced measures to encourage AI usage. Duolingo has added AI use as a performance metric for employees, while JPMorgan and Disney have introduced AI token leaderboards to track staff usage.Businesses are also exploring ways to reduce AI-related spending by using different AI models for different tasks instead of relying on a single provider. Coinbase CEO Brian Armstrong said in June that this approach helps prevent unnecessary AI token usage on simpler tasks, while Vercel CEO Guillermo Rauch recently said companies are becoming more selective about using different AI tools across their technology stack.Canva has also expanded its AI offerings for users. In April, the company introduced Canva AI 2.0, a conversational platform that allows users to create designs using text prompts.
Article Summary: Canva doesn't require employees to use a single AI chatbot. Instead, staff are free to choose the tools that best fit their work. Canva also sets aside time for employees to focus entirely on AI exploration. Some companies have introduced measures to encourage AI usage.

Article Title: Cyclist Willie Smit disqualified from race for wearing AI glasses; says: A warning, fine or yellow card could have also been ...
Article Text:
Image credit: X (Twitter)

"Today I was disqualified for the first time in my cycling career (14 years), for wearing glasses that record video.

Unfortunately I was not aware of a new rule that was implemented in April that prohibited this. A warning, fine or yellow card could have also been enough."

Smit questions difference between wearable and handheld cameras

"But what I struggle to understand is why In the Tour de France can you quite literally vlog with a camera in your hand which is perfectly legal...but because the camera is in the glasses you get an automatic Disqualification?!"

“Yes, If I knew about the rule I would have also never posted it on social media. Anyways, I'll take it on the chin,”

“Here is the video that got me disqualified & the other video is just to show what is completely allowed it seems. And lastly, my glasses have absolutely no AI capabilities unless used with a phone. So on the bike it can do nothing but record video!”

Disqualification linked to UCI onboard technology rule

Cyclist Willie Smit says he was disqualified from the opening stage of the Tour of Magnificent Qinghai after wearing AI-enabled video-recording glasses. He argued that a warning or fine would have been a more appropriate penalty for this. The incident was linked to a UCI rule introduced earlier this year governing onboard recording technology. Smit, who races for China Anta-Mentech, announced the disqualification on X after Stage 1 of the Tour of Magnificent Qinghai, a 120. 6km sprint stage in Xining. While his post referenced practices seen at the Tour de France, the disqualification itself occurred at the Tour of Magnificent Qinghai.In his X post, Smit wrote,According to the Union Cycliste Internationale (UCI), regulation 1.3.006 bis permits image-capturing devices mounted on bicycles unless a discipline specifically allows wearable devices. Video-recording glasses are not covered by the regulation, and the prescribed penalties include refusal to start, elimination, or disqualification.Following his disqualification, Smit questioned why riders are allowed to use handheld cameras in some races, while video-recording glasses result in automatic disqualification.He wrote,he added.Smit also said,His post included footage recorded using his glasses alongside a video of rider Toms Skujinš using what appeared to be a handheld camera during the Tour de France.The glasses Smit used are Oakley Meta Vanguard AI glasses, which include a built-in 12MP camera and Meta AI functionality. According to Smit, the AI features are unavailable while riding unless the glasses are connected to a phone.Stage 1 of the Tour of Magnificent Qinghai was won by Alexander Salby of Li Ning Star. Smit's result was removed following the application of the UCI's onboard technology regulation.
Article Summary: Willie Smit disqualified from opening stage of Tour of Magnificent Qinghai. Smit says he was disqualified for wearing AI-enabled video-recording glasses. Incident linked to UCI rule introduced earlier this year governing onboard recording technology. His result was removed following the application of the UCI's onboard technology regulation.

Article Title: OnePlus sister company Realme may exit China as parent company Oppo launches global shakeup
Article Text:
Realme’s new focus



‘Global fall’ of OnePlus



Realme is preparing to exit its home market of China, a report has said. Bloomberg claims that the ‘change’ is part of a consolidation plan by Realme's parent company, Oppo, which is facing financial hurdles, shifting consumer habits and intense geopolitical pressures. As part of the same corporate overhaul, Oppo is also pulling the plug on its sister brand, OnePlus, forcing it to cease all operations in the US and Europe as early as this week.While leaving China is a strategic shift for Realme, Oppo is not abandoning the brand entirely. Instead, the parent company plans to redirect its resources toward regions where it has found stronger traction. Moving forward, Oppo will focus its efforts on Central Europe and push the Realme brand into the Nordic region, which includes Denmark, Finland, Sweden and Iceland.This pivot comes as the domestic Chinese smartphone market faces a severe slowdown. According to data released by market tracker IDC, handset shipments in China dropped 4.3% in the second quarter compared to the previous year. The decline has been worsened by the surging costs of memory chips and phone components, making it incredibly difficult for manufacturers to turn a profit on the low-cost, high-value devices that Realme and OnePlus specialise in.While Realme retreats from China to focus on Europe, OnePlus is facing an even harsher reality, the report suggests. In the US, OnePlus has failed to build momentum, lagging far behind market titans Apple and Samsung, as well as smaller competitors like Google and Motorola. The brand’s recent premium launch, the OnePlus 15, suffered a rocky US introduction.Citing a person with knowledge of the private plan, OnePlus will temporarily remain active in China. However, the brand’s shutdown is scheduled to expand to the rest of the world, including its massive user base in India, at some point in 2027.
Article Summary: Realme is preparing to exit its home market of China, a report has said. It is part of a consolidation plan by Realme's parent company, Oppo. Oppo is also pulling the plug on its sister brand, OnePlus, forcing it to cease all operations in the US and Europe as early as this week.

Article Title: Mark Cuban's 'warning' to Americans: AI risks making US healthcare worse as there is not a single company that ...
Article Text:
"AI is already a better doctor than 99.99% of human doctors.”

“There isn't a single company, including yours, that knows the actual cost of the care they purchase for your employees and families,”

Read Marc Cuban’s full message about AI risks in US healthcare system



“It's not. Why? Because 25 pct or more of a doctor's time is spent dealing with conglomerates that do all they can to make the doctor's care more difficult, and expensive, for both the doctor and patient.

For every future agent we give AI doctors to deal with this friction, and to improve the quality of care, the conglomerates will have multiple adversarial agents doing all they can to delay and deny, to minimize their cost and maximize their float

We see this already as the conglomerates use AI to find every possible way to manipulate contracts, and find ways to mislead, while hospitals hire companies for Revenue Cycle Management, who charge as much as 10 pct of revenue to have their agents try to do the reverse. It's the agentic version of Mad magazine Spy vs Spy

I'll give you a further example. There isn't a single company, including yours, that knows the actual cost of the care they purchase for your employees and families. Not one.

Cost is an important component of health care decision making. @a16z includes costs in defining its benefits. But you are blind to all but the total bill you pay.

Your carrier, your ASO, your PBM, any company that touches the economics of care for your company is going to do everything they can to prevent you from using AI doctors or agents successfully

If you want to see that change, stop working with the healthcare conglomerates. Write agents that define, optimize and contract directly with providers, to eliminate the uncessary middlemen.

Feel free to use http://costpluswellness.com to train them.

Until the conglomerates are disintermediated , HC in this country will continue to be f**ked.”
Article Summary: Marc Cuban: "AI is already a better doctor than 99.99% of human doctors.” “There isn't a single company, including yours, that knows the actual cost of the care they purchase for your employees and families,” Cuban said.
